APPROPRIATENESS.justification: The paper addresses neural machine translation with a novel chunk-based decoder architecture (§3.1.2–3.3) built on standard attention-based NMT (§2), which is a central topic for top NLP/ML venues. It targets the right audience (MT/NLP researchers) and is positioned with appropriate related work citations (Bahdanau et al., Eriguchi et al., Luong et al.). The problem formulation, hierarchical decoder models, and experimental evaluation on WAT ’16/ASPEC using BLEU and RIBES with comparisons to strong baselines (seq2seq, tree-to-seq) follow the methods and evaluation standards expected at leading conferences.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper proposes a chunk-based decoder architecture (a chunk-level decoder plus a word-level decoder; §3.1.2–3.1.3) and two refinements (inter-chunk connections and word-to-chunk feedback in §3.2–3.3), which is a clear design contribution for NMT decoders. However, the idea is an incremental application of existing concepts: chunk/phrase-based translation in SMT (Watanabe et al., Kim et al.) and hierarchical/stacked RNNs in NLP and MT (Li et al., Luong & Manning, stacked RNN work cited), and prior NMT work has explored structure in encoders (Eriguchi et al. 2016b) rather than decoders. Given this, the contribution is moderately novel—noteworthy for shifting chunk modeling to the decoder, but not a fundamentally new paradigm.

IMPACT.justification: The paper tackles an important problem—capturing multi-scale structure in NMT decoders by introducing a chunk-level decoder for global (inter-chunk) dependencies and a word-level decoder for intra-chunk order (sections 3.1–3.3). Experiments report meaningful gains on a standard WAT ’16 English→Japanese task and claim the best model outperforms the best single NMT reported in WAT ’16 (results section), indicating practical benefit. However, the demonstrated gains are on a language (Japanese) with very reliable chunking (bunsetsu) and the approach is an incremental architectural refinement rather than a paradigm shift, so its likely impact is moderate: useful and extendable but not field-transforming.

CLARITY.justification: The paper has a generally sensible high-level organization (intro → methods → experiments → results) but the provided text contains many duplicated paragraphs, LaTeX/TeX artifacts, and broken repetitions that interrupt flow (e.g., multiple repeated sentences in the Introduction/Related Work and training sections). Notation is inconsistently presented (the attention weight formula α_{ji} uses an incorrect/ambiguous normalization index in Eq. (13)), and several figure/table references are unhelpful because the figures/tables are not present or are only referenced (Figure 4, Figure 3, partial system table). Overall the writing suffers from clarity and proofreading problems (grammar and formatting issues, stray citations and OCR-like repeats) that require substantial cleanup to be easily readable.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The paper provides a reasonable experimental evaluation on the ASPEC English–Japanese task (2M training pairs, BLEU and RIBES; compared to word-based and tree-to-sequence baselines) and studies three incremental model variants (Model 1→Model 2→Model 3), which acts as an internal ablation. However, experiments are limited to a single language pair/dataset, there is no broader sensitivity analysis (e.g. effect of chunking errors, chunk size, or hyperparameter sweeps) and failure cases/limitations are not discussed beyond brief future work. Reproducibility is only partial: some details are given (GRU, ADADELTA, vocab sizes, beam search, bunsetsu chunker), but many training/architecture hyperparameters and exact preprocessing/alignment steps are not fully specified.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper compares to appropriate recent baselines (word-based seq2seq (Li et al., 2016), tree-to-seq models of Eriguchi et al. (2016a,b)) and reports results on the ASPEC/WAT’16 task using BLEU and RIBES, claiming improvements over the best single NMT at WAT’16. It follows WAT’16 evaluation and training-data settings, but the results table shows differing model configurations (RNN types and vocab sizes for compared systems), suggesting some potential inconsistency in experimental conditions. The authors explicitly state when prior models are outperformed and relate their design to prior hierarchical/tree encoders, though deeper analysis/ablations explaining the empirical gains is limited. Overall the comparisons are good and relevant but have minor fairness/analysis omissions.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper states clear claims and reports BLEU/RIBES improvements on ASPEC against reasonable baselines (§4 and result table), so there is experimental support, but methodological details are incomplete in the provided text (e.g. how target chunk boundaries are obtained/trained is not described). The mathematical model definitions for the chunk- and word-level GRU updates are plausible, but the attention softmax is written incorrectly in Eq.(13) (denominator sums over j' instead of over source positions i'), indicating a correctness/sloppiness issue. Experimental methodology omits important details (chunk supervision, full hyperparameters, statistical significance tests), so while results are suggestive the arguments have logical gaps and missing evidence to fully justify the claimed improvements.
